subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis gravidarum admission and thiamine before glucose
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in the emergency department reviewing a 26-year-old G2P1 at 9+0 weeks in her second pregnancy who presents with three weeks of intractable nausea, 14 vomits in the last 24 hours and inability to tolerate oral fluids. She has lost 6 kg since her booking weight 4 weeks ago (8% of pre-pregnancy body weight). Pyridoxine, doxylamine and metoclopramide tablets at home have not helped. She has not taken any thiamine supplementation. She has no fever, abdominal pain or per vaginal bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg supine, HR 112, T 36.6 °C, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 sec, mildly ketotic breath, soft non-tender abdomen, no localising signs
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 3.2, Cl 92, urea 8.6, creatinine 78, glucose 3.6 mmol/L, urinary ketones 3+, beta-hCG appropriate for 9 weeks, TFTs pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Commence 5% dextrose 1 L over 2 hours, add ondansetron 4 mg IV every 8 hours and arrange discharge home with oral pyridoxine, doxylamine and antenatal dietitian follow-up within the next week
options.rationale: Giving dextrose before thiamine in a thiamine-replete-unknown patient with prolonged vomiting risks precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; ketosis and electrolyte derangement also mandate IV rehydration first. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Administer one litre of Hartmann's solution over 1 hour, give IM metoclopramide 10 mg every 8 hours, and discharge once tolerating oral fluids without specific thiamine or potassium replacement
options.rationale: Hypokalaemia at 3.2 mmol/L requires correction and any patient with prolonged vomiting needs thiamine before any glucose-containing fluid. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Admit for IV normal saline with added potassium chloride to correct dehydration, give IV thiamine 100 mg daily before any glucose-containing fluid, and use ondansetron or metoclopramide as antiemetic with pyridoxine and doxylamine continued
options.rationale: PUQE-equivalent severe HG with weight loss over 5%, ketonuria, hypokalaemia and hyponatraemia warrants admission; thiamine 100 mg IV is given before glucose, and saline-with-potassium replaces losses. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Admit for total parenteral nutrition via a peripherally inserted central catheter for 7 days, withhold all oral intake and stop pyridoxine and doxylamine while parenteral nutrition is in place to allow gut rest
options.rationale: TPN is reserved for refractory cases unable to maintain weight despite enteral feeding; first-line is rehydration, electrolyte and thiamine replacement with antiemetics. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

options.text: Arrange urgent ultrasound to exclude molar pregnancy and multiple gestation, withhold antiemetics until imaging is complete and refer to early pregnancy assessment for repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours before further treatment
options.rationale: Imaging for molar or multiple pregnancy is appropriate but does not delay rehydration, thiamine and antiemetic therapy in a haemodynamically unwell patient. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is defined by intractable vomiting, weight loss over 5% of pre-pregnancy weight, ketosis and electrolyte disturbance. Initial management is admission for IV crystalloid (typically saline with added potassium chloride), parenteral antiemetics (metoclopramide, ondansetron, prochlorperazine) and thiamine 100 mg IV daily before any glucose-containing fluid to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Pyridoxine plus doxylamine remains first-line outpatient therapy. Ultrasound is arranged to exclude molar pregnancy or multiple gestation if not already done. Refractory cases need corticosteroids or admission for nutritional support.
key_points: HG diagnosis: vomiting + over 5% weight loss + ketosis + electrolyte disturbance
Thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid to prevent Wernicke
Replace K aggressively if under 3.5; saline + KCl
Pyridoxine + doxylamine first-line outpatient; ondansetron/metoclopramide if needed
Refractory: corticosteroids, TPN, ultrasound to exclude molar
explanation.pearls: Confusion, ataxia or ophthalmoplegia in a vomiting pregnant woman is Wernicke encephalopathy until proven otherwise; give 500 mg thiamine IV TDS for 3 days, then 250 mg daily, before any dextrose - the cost of missing this is permanent Korsakoff amnesia.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum

subtopic_detail: Antiphospholipid syndrome confirmation and aspirin plus LMWH management
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in a preconception clinic seeing a 35-year-old woman who has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages at 8, 10 and 9 weeks gestation, each with documented fetal pole and absent fetal heart on follow-up ultrasound. She has no living children and no prior pregnancy losses beyond 12 weeks. She is otherwise well, takes folic acid and iodine. Investigations after her second loss showed normal karyotype on both partners, normal pelvic ultrasound, normal thyroid function and HbA1c. Lupus anticoagulant was positive on a single sample. Repeat testing 12 weeks later again shows positive lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies are negative on both occasions.

data_table.Investigations: Both LA samples positive 12 weeks apart, ACL and B2GP1 negative both times, FBC and renal function normal, no prior thrombosis

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan once she conceives again?

options.text: Commence warfarin 5 mg daily before conception and continue throughout pregnancy with a target INR of 2.0 to 3.0 to minimise placental microthrombi formation and recurrent first-trimester loss
options.rationale: Warfarin is teratogenic between 6 and 12 weeks gestation (warfarin embryopathy) and is contraindicated in early pregnancy in obstetric APS. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management

options.text: Confirm obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome on her recurrent loss pattern and persistent lupus anticoagulant, then once pregnancy is confirmed commence low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily and prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily
options.rationale: Three or more first-trimester losses plus persistent LA on two occasions 12 weeks apart meets Sydney/Sapporo classification for obstetric APS; treatment is low-dose aspirin from positive pregnancy test plus prophylactic LMWH. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management

options.text: Recommend natural killer cell immunotherapy with intravenous immunoglobulin every 4 weeks from confirmation of pregnancy until 28 weeks gestation to suppress maternal alloimmune rejection of the embryo
options.rationale: NK-cell immunotherapy and IVIG are not supported by evidence for recurrent miscarriage and are not recommended in current Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management

options.text: Counsel that no specific treatment is indicated because she does not meet criteria for obstetric APS without anticardiolipin or anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibody positivity in addition to her lupus anticoagulant
options.rationale: Persistent LA alone on two samples 12 weeks apart with three first-trimester losses meets obstetric APS criteria; treatment is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management

options.text: Commence high-dose prednisolone 40 mg daily and aspirin 100 mg daily from a positive pregnancy test and continue at this dose until 20 weeks gestation to suppress autoimmune placental injury
options.rationale: High-dose prednisolone is associated with gestational diabetes, hypertension and preterm birth without improving live-birth rate in APS and is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management

explanation.summary: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome is defined by recurrent pregnancy loss (3 or more under 10 weeks, or 1 or more after 10 weeks of morphologically normal fetus, or 1 or more preterm birth before 34 weeks due to severe pre-eclampsia or placental insufficiency) plus persistent positive antiphospholipid antibodies on two samples 12 weeks apart. Lupus anticoagulant alone is sufficient. Treatment in pregnancy is low-dose aspirin from positive pregnancy test plus prophylactic LMWH until 6 weeks postpartum. Live birth rates improve from around 10% untreated to 70 to 80% with aspirin plus LMWH.
key_points: Obstetric APS: 3+ losses under 10 weeks, OR 1+ loss after 10 weeks, OR 1+ severe early-PET preterm birth
PLUS persistent aPL on 2 samples 12 weeks apart (LA, ACL, or B2GP1)
Treatment: aspirin 100 mg daily + enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily through pregnancy
Continue LMWH 6 weeks postpartum
Warfarin contraindicated 6-12 weeks (embryopathy)
explanation.pearls: Triple-positive APS (LA + ACL + B2GP1) carries the highest thrombotic and pregnancy risk; this patient has only single-positive LA but persistent positivity twice still meets criteria - do not require all three to make the diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage investigation and management
ESHRE Guideline: Recurrent pregnancy loss 2022 update

subtopic_detail: Heterotopic pregnancy after IVF with intrauterine and adnexal sac
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in the early pregnancy assessment clinic seeing a 34-year-old woman at 7+3 weeks by IVF transfer date who reports increasing right iliac fossa pain over the last 48 hours and a small amount of dark per vaginal bleeding today. She is haemodynamically stable. The pregnancy was conceived via fresh embryo transfer (two blastocysts) 51 days ago. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an intrauterine gestational sac with a 6+5 fetal pole and cardiac activity (CRL 11 mm), free fluid in the pouch of Douglas, and a separate right adnexal complex mass measuring 28 mm with no obvious yolk sac but a hyperechoic ring surrounding a small fluid collection.

data_table.Examination: Right iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no peritonism, closed cervix on speculum, scant dark blood
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG 24,000 IU/L, Hb 118 g/L, FBC otherwise normal, group A positive antibody negative

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 to treat the right adnexal pregnancy while observing the intrauterine pregnancy expectantly with serial beta-hCG monitoring every 48 hours and ultrasound at 1 week
options.rationale: Methotrexate would terminate the viable intrauterine pregnancy and is contraindicated in heterotopic pregnancy where the intrauterine pregnancy is viable and desired. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

options.text: Arrange repeat ultrasound and beta-hCG in 48 hours with safety-net advice for emergency presentation if pain or bleeding worsen, on the basis that the adnexal mass may represent a corpus luteum cyst rather than a coexistent ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: Symptomatic right iliac fossa pain with free fluid and an adnexal ring complex in an IVF pregnancy is heterotopic until proven otherwise; deferring intervention 48 hours risks rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

options.text: Arrange ultrasound-guided transvaginal aspiration of the adnexal mass and instillation of potassium chloride 2 mmol into the ectopic sac, preserving the intrauterine pregnancy with no need for laparoscopic intervention
options.rationale: KCl instillation is a second-line technique for non-tubal heterotopic pregnancies (cornual, cervical, caesarean scar) and is not the first-line approach for symptomatic tubal heterotopic with peritoneal fluid. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

options.text: Arrange urgent laparoscopic salpingectomy of the right tubal ectopic pregnancy under general anaesthesia, manipulating the uterus minimally and counselling that the intrauterine pregnancy will most likely continue uneventfully
options.rationale: Symptomatic tubal heterotopic pregnancy with free peritoneal fluid in a haemodynamically stable woman is managed by laparoscopic salpingectomy under GA with minimal uterine manipulation; methotrexate cannot be used due to the coexisting viable IUP. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

options.text: Plan suction evacuation of the intrauterine pregnancy in theatre followed by single-dose methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 for the ectopic pregnancy given the high risk of miscarriage of the intrauterine pregnancy after laparoscopy
options.rationale: Sacrificing a viable, desired intrauterine pregnancy is not justified; published IVF heterotopic series report ongoing IUP rates over 60% after laparoscopic salpingectomy. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

explanation.summary: Heterotopic pregnancy is rare in spontaneous conceptions (1 in 30,000) but rises to around 1 in 100 after IVF, particularly with multiple embryo transfer. Diagnosis hinges on visualising an intrauterine pregnancy AND a separate adnexal mass; serial beta-hCG is unhelpful because trends are confounded by the viable IUP. Management of a symptomatic tubal heterotopic in a stable patient is laparoscopic salpingectomy with minimal uterine manipulation. Methotrexate is contraindicated when the IUP is desired. Reported live-birth rate of the IUP after surgical management is 60 to 70%.
key_points: Heterotopic risk: 1 in 30,000 spontaneous, 1 in 100 after IVF
Serial beta-hCG cannot diagnose - rely on ultrasound visualisation
Methotrexate contraindicated if IUP is desired
Laparoscopic salpingectomy is first-line for symptomatic tubal heterotopic
KCl injection is option for cornual, cervical, scar heterotopic
explanation.pearls: After IVF embryo transfer, persistent unilateral pain or unexplained free fluid in the presence of a normal-appearing IUP should trigger careful adnexal sweep on TVS - the intrauterine sac is reassuring on autopilot but does not rule out a co-located ectopic.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

subtopic_detail: NCSP management of oncogenic HPV 16/18 positive primary screening test
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman who had her routine Cervical Screening Test 4 weeks ago. The result reports oncogenic HPV detected, type 16, with reflex liquid-based cytology showing low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL). She has had two prior normal screening tests at age 25 and 27, is in a monogamous relationship, has never had an abnormal cervical screen and is not pregnant. She is not immunocompromised. She has no symptoms; specifically no postcoital bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding or vaginal discharge.

data_table.Examination: Speculum exam at sample collection 4 weeks ago: cervix appears macroscopically normal
data_table.Investigations: CST result as above: HPV 16 positive, reflex cytology LSIL; previous CSTs negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months because cytology shows only LSIL and most low-grade lesions regress spontaneously without colposcopy or further intervention being required for screening management
options.rationale: HPV 16 or 18 detection on the primary screen is referred directly to colposcopy regardless of cytology because of the higher progression risk associated with type 16. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024

options.text: Arrange repeat cervical screening test in 6 months together with self-collected HPV swab to confirm persistence before referring her for colposcopy and biopsy at a tertiary gynaecology oncology centre
options.rationale: HPV 16/18 positive on primary CST is referred to colposcopy without further repeat HPV testing; self-collection is a primary screening option, not a triage tool. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024

options.text: Treat empirically with a course of cervical cryotherapy in clinic to ablate any low-grade lesion, then repeat the cervical screening test in 6 months to confirm resolution of the HPV 16 infection
options.rationale: Treating without histological diagnosis is not appropriate; colposcopy with directed biopsy is required to confirm or exclude high-grade dysplasia before any ablative or excisional therapy. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024

options.text: Arrange counselling about HPV transmission and vaccinate her with the 9-valent HPV vaccine catch-up dose series, then repeat screening in 5 years if vaccination response is documented as adequate by serology testing
options.rationale: HPV vaccination is not used to manage existing HPV infection or to alter screening intervals in already-infected patients. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024

options.text: Refer to colposcopy for direct visualisation of the cervix and directed biopsy as needed because oncogenic HPV type 16 detection on primary screening is a higher-risk result regardless of accompanying reflex cytology grade
options.rationale: Under the 2024 NCSP, HPV 16 or 18 on the primary screen is referred directly to colposcopy; the cytology grade does not alter the referral pathway because of type 16's elevated progression risk. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024

explanation.summary: The NCSP replaced Pap smears with primary HPV testing every 5 years from 2017 for women aged 25 to 74. The reflex pathway depends on whether HPV 16 or 18 (higher risk types) are detected. Any HPV 16 or 18 positive is referred to colposcopy regardless of cytology. Other oncogenic types ("oncogenic non-16/18" - types 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58, 59, 68) are triaged by reflex cytology: normal or LSIL goes to repeat HPV at 12 months, HSIL or higher goes to colposcopy. Self-collection is now offered as the primary screening method to all eligible women.
key_points: NCSP: primary HPV every 5 years, ages 25-74
HPV 16/18 positive: refer to colposcopy regardless of cytology
Oncogenic non-16/18 + normal/LSIL: repeat HPV at 12 months
Oncogenic non-16/18 + HSIL or higher: colposcopy
Self-collection now equivalent to clinician-collected screening
explanation.pearls: Co-test (HPV + cytology) is reserved for surveillance of treated high-grade lesions; for primary screening in 2024 the HPV result drives the pathway and the cytology is reflex - reading the LSIL but ignoring the HPV 16 reverses the decision logic.

sources.label: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program: Guidelines 2024
Cancer Council Australia: Cervical cancer prevention and screening

subtopic_detail: Failed first-line hormonal therapy and indication for diagnostic laparoscopy with excision
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 27-year-old nulligravid woman with 4 years of cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia and ovulatory pain. She has had a 12-month trial of combined oral contraceptive pill taken continuously without break, with adequate compliance, and a 6-month trial of norethisterone 5 mg twice daily added subsequently. Her pain numerical rating score remains 7 to 8 out of 10 on most days with 3 to 4 days of school absence per month. She has not yet attempted pregnancy and is not currently trying to conceive. Examination shows a tender uterosacral ligament nodule and a fixed retroverted uterus. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma and a hypoechoic mass at the rectovaginal septum.

data_table.Examination: Tender uterosacral nodule, fixed retroverted uterus, no palpable mass on bimanual
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic US: 3 cm left endometrioma, suspected rectovaginal deep infiltrating endometriosis; CA-125 elevated at 76; STI screen negative

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Plan diagnostic laparoscopy with excision of visible endometriotic deposits at a unit with bowel and ureteric expertise, after preoperative imaging staging and multidisciplinary planning for possible rectovaginal excision
options.rationale: Failed adequate hormonal therapy plus deep infiltrating endometriosis features on examination and imaging is the standard threshold for diagnostic laparoscopy with planned excision at a tertiary unit. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (leuprorelin 3.75 mg monthly) with tibolone add-back for 12 months because medical menopause is the most effective second-line therapy and avoids surgical risk in young nulligravid women
options.rationale: GnRH agonist is a reasonable medical second-line for some women but does not address mechanical deep infiltrating disease causing fixed pelvic anatomy; surgical excision is recommended where DIE is suspected. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel-releasing 52 mg intrauterine device as third-line hormonal therapy and review symptom response in 6 months before considering surgical investigation or referral to a tertiary endometriosis service
options.rationale: LNG-IUS can help with pain in selected patients but is unlikely to control symptoms driven by deep infiltrating disease with rectovaginal nodule, and delays definitive diagnosis and management. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard

options.text: Recommend a definitive total laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy because of severity, failed medical therapy and presence of deep infiltrating disease confirmed on ultrasound and examination
options.rationale: Definitive surgery with BSO in a 27-year-old nulligravid woman is not appropriate before fertility-sparing excisional options are tried; conservation of ovarian function is a priority. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard

options.text: Reassure her that elevated CA-125 with an endometrioma is typical and continue current hormonal therapy with the addition of regular pelvic floor physiotherapy and a multidisciplinary pain clinic referral instead of surgical management
options.rationale: Multidisciplinary care including physio and pain services is valuable but does not replace excisional surgery for symptomatic deep infiltrating disease after failed medical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Endometriosis clinical care standard

explanation.summary: Endometriosis is managed in a stepped approach. First-line medical therapy is COCP (cyclic or continuous) or progestogen (norethisterone, dienogest) for pain. LNG-IUS and GnRH agonist with add-back are second-line. Surgical management with excision (preferred over ablation) is indicated when medical therapy fails, when deep infiltrating disease is suspected on imaging or examination, when there is symptomatic endometrioma over 3 cm, or when fertility is desired. Surgery for deep disease involving bowel, ureter or rectovaginal septum should occur in tertiary units with multidisciplinary expertise. CA-125 lacks specificity but is often elevated in DIE.
key_points: First-line medical: continuous COCP or progestogen
Second-line medical: LNG-IUS or GnRH agonist with add-back
Surgical excision: failed medical therapy, DIE, endometrioma over 3 cm, infertility
Excision preferred over ablation for recurrence rates
DIE surgery requires tertiary unit with bowel/ureter expertise
explanation.pearls: Endometrioma surgery in women planning pregnancy must weigh recurrence and adhesion benefits against AMH and ovarian reserve loss from cystectomy; in women with deep infiltrating disease who want fertility, multidisciplinary discussion before any operation is essential.
